metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `signing.py`'s `_gate_commit_sha` (and `gate_code_version`'s docstring) to this plan's scope so the live-git path also emits full 40-char SHAs, or drop the 'canonical full SHA' requirement and keep both paths on short SHAs — do not leave the two paths emitting different-length SHAs with no test asserting the format is consistent.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'any `signing.py` display/match code is confirmed to accept the full SHA', but `signing.py` is built around SHORT SHAs throughout: `_gate_commit_sha` calls `git rev-parse --short HEAD`, its docstring says 'Short commit SHA'; `gate_code_version`'s docstring says `<short-sha>[-dirty]`; `rebar_version_step`'s docstring says `<short-sha>[-dirty]`. The plan proposes changing `hatch_build.py`'s git fallback to full SHA but does not mention changing `signing.py`'s live-git path — creating a format skew where the baked SHA (full 40-char) differs from the live-checkout SHA (short) in the same `rebar-version:` manifest line format.
findings.location: What section — 'Canonical commit format = full 40-char SHA' paragraph: 'any `signing.py` display/match code is confirmed to accept the full SHA'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/signing.py` to the Scope section; change `_gate_commit_sha` to call `git rev-parse HEAD` (no `--short`) and update its docstring. Alternatively, keep both paths on short SHAs and change the env-var value to a short SHA (e.g., `${{ github.sha | cut -c1-7 }}`), but that breaks the 'confirmed 40-char' claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/signing.py` to the Scope section and specify whether `_gate_commit_sha()` should also be updated from `--short HEAD` to full `HEAD` (to match the new baked format), or document explicitly why the short-vs-full mix between live and baked paths is acceptable.
findings.finding: The plan changes `hatch_build.py` to bake a full 40-char SHA (from `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` / `git rev-parse HEAD`) but does not include `src/rebar/signing.py` in the Scope, even though `_gate_commit_sha()` there still calls `git rev-parse --short HEAD` — meaning a wheel-installed rebar on a git-present machine would mix a full-SHA baked value (from `_baked_commit_sha()`) with a short-SHA live git value (from `_gate_commit_sha()`), producing inconsistent `gate_code_version()` output depending on install type.
findings.location: What section ('Canonical commit format = full 40-char SHA') / src/rebar/signing.py:300-305 / Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC3 to require the proving test to invoke `python -m build` (or equivalent subprocess) end-to-end in a temp directory so that the sdist-extraction and wheel-from-sdist sub-build actually execute, rather than allowing a direct `WheelBuilder` instantiation to satisfy the criterion.
findings.finding: AC3 says 'a test asserts the wheel built via the default sdist→wheel path contains COMMIT equal to that SHA' and assigns it to `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py`, but the body explicitly warns 'rather than asserting propagation as settled fact' and demands a test that 'runs `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=<40-char-hex> python -m build`'. A unit test in that file exercising a direct `WheelBuilder` call (the current pattern in lines 202-227) can satisfy the AC letter without ever invoking `python -m build` end-to-end, leaving the sdist→wheel subprocess path untested — exactly the defect the plan aims to close.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; What section — 'Verify env-var propagation' bullet; `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC3 proving clause: 'A test (may live in `tests/integration/` or as a separate fixture) invokes `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=<40-char-hex> python -m build --no-isolation --outdir <tmpdir>` in a temp directory, unpacks the resulting `.whl`, imports `_build_info`, and asserts `COMMIT == <sha>`; a direct WheelBuilder call does NOT satisfy this criterion.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define in the plan exactly what 'unresolvable' means for a non-empty REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT value — e.g. 'the hook validates the value matches /^[0-9a-f]{40}$/ and raises BuildError if it does not' — so the implementer has an unambiguous validation predicate.
findings.finding: The plan's new fail-fast rule — 'the hook raises (fails the build) only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but empty/unresolvable' — introduces a decision protocol into hatch_build.py that is never fully specified. 'Empty' is clear (the env var is set to ''), but 'unresolvable' is undefined: the env var's value is not passed to any resolver (it is used directly as the SHA string), so 'unresolvable' has no obvious meaning unless the plan intends validation (e.g. must be 40-char hex). The hook's current code writes whatever commit value it obtains without validation, and the plan does not define what constitutes an invalid non-empty value.
findings.location: What section — 'Fail-fast scoped to the release context' bullet; AC #3 — 'The hook fails the build only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but unresolvable'; hatch_build.py:initialize()
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Fail-fast' bullet: 'Validation: if REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set, the hook checks that its value matches the regex ^[0-9a-f]{40}$ (a full SHA); any other non-empty value (e.g. empty string, "undefined", partial hex) raises BuildError("REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT must be a 40-char hex SHA, got: {value!r}"). An unset env var (os.environ.get returns None) skips validation and falls back to git.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify re-run semantics: either (a) the build job is idempotent under re-run (e.g., by using `--no-build-isolation` + deterministic build flags to produce bit-identical outputs for the same commit), (b) the workflow enforces that only a full workflow re-run is permissible (not individual job re-runs), or (c) the artifact is keyed by `github.run_id + github.sha` and test jobs always download by that same key so re-runs produce a fresh, self-consistent chain rather than silently mixing validated and re-built artifacts.
findings.finding: Workflow re-run idempotency is unaddressed: if the build job is re-triggered (e.g., transient runner failure forces a retry), it produces a new artifact upload under the same name, potentially with different SHA-256 hashes (non-deterministic build timestamps in wheel metadata). Test jobs that already passed against the original artifact hashes would have validated bytes that differ from the re-built artifact, breaking the build-once guarantee and making the SHA256SUMS gate meaningless on re-runs.
findings.location: ## What — 'Publish the same bytes' bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: Key the artifact name with `${{ github.run_id }}` (e.g., `rebar-dist-${{ github.run_id }}`); test and publish jobs reference the same run-scoped name, ensuring they always consume the artifact from their own build job and a re-run produces a new, self-consistent chain rather than overwriting a previously-validated artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the Scope or a dependency edge whether this story adds `--no-isolation` to the release build command itself, or whether it has a hard prerequisite on Finding 4 being applied first — and add an AC that verifies env-var propagation via an end-to-end `python -m build` invocation (not a direct WheelBuilder call) to confirm the variable survives PEP 517 subprocess boundaries.
findings.finding: The plan states env-var propagation into the Hatchling hook during the sdist→wheel sub-build is 'guaranteed because Finding 4 builds with `--no-isolation`', but this plan's own scope and ACs contain no explicit prerequisite edge on Finding 4, no statement that `--no-isolation` will be added by this story, and no fallback if Finding 4 is not applied concurrently. An executor implementing this story independently would encounter propagation failure without knowing why.
findings.location: What section — 'Verify env-var propagation (do not assume)' bullet; Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to Scope: 'This story adds `--no-isolation` to the `python -m build` invocation in `release.yml`; Finding 4 is a hard prerequisite (or this story subsumes that flag).' Then update AC3 to mandate: 'A dedicated test invokes `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=<sha> python -m build --no-isolation` in a temp dir, extracts the resulting wheel, and asserts `COMMIT == <sha>` — not a direct WheelBuilder call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite in the plan (and a corresponding AC guard) stating that `python -m build --no-isolation` (or equivalent isolation-bypass) MUST be in place before REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT propagation is relied upon, either by absorbing that requirement into this story's scope or declaring a hard dependency on Finding 4 landing first.
findings.finding: The env-var propagation guarantee ('guaranteed because Finding 4 builds with --no-isolation') is stated as a settled fact in the 'What' section, but Finding 4 (pinning story) is explicitly scoped as a *separate* story with only 'coordinate' mentioned in this plan's Scope. If this story is implemented without Finding 4, PEP 517 build isolation will block REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT from reaching the Hatchling hook inside the extracted sdist build, silently yielding COMMIT=None again — the exact defect being fixed.
findings.location: What section ('Verify env-var propagation') and Scope section ('coordinate with Findings 1 and 4')
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `--no-isolation` to this story's own release.yml changes and AC1 (removing the dependency on Finding 4 for correctness), or (b) add an AC like 'The build step uses --no-isolation or an equivalent mechanism that allows REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT to reach the Hatchling hook' and make Finding 4 a stated prerequisite gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading Finding 4's plan or the updated release.yml) that `--no-isolation` is actually present in the merged workflow before this plan ships, OR add `--no-isolation` (or `--no-build-isolation`) explicitly to this plan's own release.yml edits and document that it is this story's responsibility — do not treat a cross-story dependency as settled fact.
findings.finding: The plan asserts as settled fact that 'on the release path [env-var propagation] is guaranteed because Finding 4 builds with `--no-isolation`', but no `--no-isolation` flag exists in any current workflow file, and the referenced 'Finding 4' plan document is not accessible to verify this cross-story dependency. This is a committed-work dependency on an unverifiable cross-story assertion.
findings.location: What section — 'Verify env-var propagation' paragraph: 'on the release path this is guaranteed because Finding 4 builds with --no-isolation (no PEP 517 subprocess isolation at all)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `python -m build --no-isolation` (or `python -m build -n`) to the build step in this plan's release.yml changes, or add a test that verifies the env var reaches the hook through the default isolated build mode (e.g., by running `python -m build` without `--no-isolation` in a temp dir with the env var set and checking the resulting wheel).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make this story self-sufficient: either add `--no-isolation` (or `--no-build-isolation`) to the `python -m build` invocation in this plan's own `release.yml` scope, or add an explicit step in the build job that verifies env var propagation reached the wheel (e.g., `unzip -p dist/*.whl '*/rebar/_build_info.py' | grep COMMIT`) before any reliance on Finding 4's landing.
findings.finding: The env-var propagation guarantee is load-bearing but cross-story: the plan states propagation is 'guaranteed because Finding 4 builds with `--no-isolation`', yet `--no-isolation` is not in this plan's scope. If this story lands before Finding 4, or if the two stories are decoupled, `python -m build` runs in default isolated mode and `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` may not reach the Hatchling `initialize()` hook during the sdist→wheel sub-build — exactly the failure mode the plan is trying to fix. The plan explicitly says 'do not assume — prove it with a test', but the proving test only asserts the RESULT (wheel carries the SHA), not the precondition (isolation mode). A mid-flight landing order where this story's hook change is live but Finding 4's `--no-isolation` is not yet merged would silently produce `COMMIT = None` again.
findings.location: What section — 'Verify env-var propagation (do not assume)' bullet; Scope — 'coordinate with Findings 1 and 4'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--no-isolation` to the `python -m build` call in this plan's release.yml build step (the plan already notes pypa/build inherits env vars when isolation is disabled), making the mechanism self-contained. The Scope section already touches release.yml, so this is within scope. The AC #3 test that asserts the wheel-from-sdist path should also be updated to run with `--no-isolation` so it validates the same code path used in the release workflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the circular 'guaranteed because Finding 4' assertion with a codebase-grounded or doc-cited confirmation that `pypa/build` (in its actual invocation mode for this story) propagates host env vars into the Hatchling backend subprocess during the sdist→wheel build; if `--no-isolation` is required, explicitly add it to this story's scope rather than deferring to Finding 4.
findings.finding: The plan's core fix — `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` reaching the Hatchling `initialize()` hook during the sdist→wheel sub-build — rests on an unverified capability assertion about `pypa/build`'s env-var propagation through PEP 517 isolation. The plan simultaneously acknowledges uncertainty ('do not assume') and asserts it is 'guaranteed because Finding 4 builds with `--no-isolation`' — but `--no-isolation` is Finding 4's scope, not this story's, making the guarantee circular and ungrounded at design time.
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the hook' / 'Verify env-var propagation' subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--no-isolation` (or `-n`) to `python -m build` in the build job spec within this story's scope (it does not need to wait for Finding 4), and cite the pypa/build source or docs confirming that with `--no-isolation` the backend runs in-process / inherits env. Then the propagation guarantee is locally grounded and testable.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'optionally add' in the `test.yml` Scope bullet with a firm decision (e.g., 'Out of scope for this story — tracked as a follow-up' or 'In scope: add the probe to `test.yml` so the defect is caught in regular CI') so the executor doesn't have to choose.
findings.finding: The Scope bullet for `test.yml` reads 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI so the defect is caught pre-release' — 'optionally' is an unranked placeholder that defers the in/out decision to the executor without a stated default.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/workflows/test.yml` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: 'Out of scope for this story (tracked as a follow-up): adding the wheel/sdist probe to `test.yml` so the defect is caught pre-release.' Or: 'In scope: add a wheel/sdist build-and-probe step to `test.yml` that runs on every PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete rollback/back-out step for the short→full SHA format change: either (a) audit and enumerate all COMMIT consumers in the codebase with a grep result in the plan, (b) add an AC that `grep -r '_build_info.COMMIT'` shows no short-SHA-specific parsing, or (c) describe an expand-contract sequence (ship full SHA, deprecate short-SHA branch) if external consumers exist.
findings.finding: The short→full SHA format change to `_build_info.COMMIT` is acknowledged as an intentional behavioral change, but the plan provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequence. The plan asserts 'signing.py display/match code is confirmed to accept the full SHA' without defining what 'confirmed' means or what to do if an external consumer of COMMIT relies on the short format.
findings.location: What section ('Canonical commit format = full 40-char SHA') and hatch_build.py scope bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line grep audit to the plan body: `grep -rn 'COMMIT' src/ tests/` and list all call sites, then assert each accepts a 40-char hex string. Include this as a checklist item in the AC so reviewers can verify it at merge time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit tests in `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py` (or a dedicated hook test) for the `hatch_build.py` fail-fast branch: one test asserting the hook raises (build error) when `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` is set to an empty string, and one when set to a non-hex/unresolvable value, so the error path is covered alongside the happy-path AC3 test.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new fail-fast branch in `hatch_build.py` (raise when `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` is set but empty or unresolvable) but specifies no test — unit or integration — that exercises this error path. The acceptance criteria and scope only call out coverage for the happy path (COMMIT equals the SHA) and the formerly-failing sdist→wheel path; the error branch is uncovered.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 / hatch_build.py / Scope item 'tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two parametrized test cases to the hook test: `@pytest.mark.parametrize('val', ['', 'not-a-sha'])` patching `os.environ['REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT']` and asserting the hook raises a descriptive exception; include this in the AC3 bullet as a required test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `github_release` and `mcp_registry` to the Scope section as consumers of the `dist` artifact bundle that require coordinated review when the bundle name, structure, or `publish`'s `needs:` chain changes.
findings.finding: The `github_release` and `mcp_registry` jobs in `release.yml` both download the artifact bundle using `actions/download-artifact@v8` with `name: dist` and consume `dist/*` directly. The plan restructures this bundle (new SHA256SUMS file, potentially new artifact name, changed `needs:` chain) but neither job appears in the Scope section or the What section as a consumer requiring coordinated update — they are unenumerated callers of the artifact that will silently fail or pick up unexpected files.
findings.location: Scope section / .github/workflows/release.yml lines 75-108 (github_release), 109-179 (mcp_registry)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `release.yml`, append: 'also update `github_release` (downloads `name: dist` for `gh release upload`) and `mcp_registry` (`needs: publish`) to account for any artifact name change and for the SHA256SUMS file appearing in `dist/`; filter or exclude SHA256SUMS from `gh release upload dist/*` if it should not appear as a release asset.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate `github_release` and `mcp_registry` as affected consumers in the Scope section, and specify how they download the restructured bundle (updated artifact name, whether SHA256SUMS is excluded from the GitHub Release asset upload, etc.).
findings.finding: The `github_release` and `mcp_registry` jobs in the existing `release.yml` both download the artifact by name (`name: dist`, as seen at lines 86 and implicitly via `needs: publish`). The plan restructures the artifact bundle (adding SHA256SUMS, potentially renaming it) but does not list `github_release` or `mcp_registry` as consumers that need updating. If the bundle is renamed or its contents change structure, those downstream jobs will silently fail at download-time or pick up unexpected artifacts — breaking the release atomicity the plan is trying to guarantee.
findings.location: Scope section — '.github/workflows/release.yml — build-once + two artifact-test jobs + hash-gated publish'; existing release.yml lines 85-108 (github_release job) and lines 109-179 (mcp_registry job)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--no-isolation` (or equivalent) to this story's own `python -m build` invocation in `release.yml` (don't rely solely on Finding 4 owning it), OR add an explicit AC that gates merging this story on Finding 4's `--no-isolation` contract being present in the same release.yml edit — so the env-var propagation guarantee is owned within this plan's scope.
findings.finding: The plan's primary fix mechanism — REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT env var propagating through python -m build's PEP 517 isolated subprocess into the Hatchling hook during the sdist→wheel sub-build — is a new capability assertion whose guarantee is deferred to 'Finding 4 builds with --no-isolation', a cross-story dependency outside this plan's scope and AC set. If Finding 4 ships after or independently, this plan's own release path could run with default isolation and silently produce COMMIT = None again. The plan's own AC3 requires a test that 'asserts the wheel built via the default sdist→wheel path contains COMMIT equal to that SHA', but the test alone does not substitute for owning the --no-isolation contract in this story's scope.
findings.location: What section — 'Verify env-var propagation' bullet; Scope section — 'coordinate with Findings 1 and 4'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's 'Fix the hook' bullet, change 'the workflow sets REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }} before python -m build' to 'the workflow sets REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }} before python -m build --no-isolation', and add to Scope that this story's release.yml edit includes --no-isolation so the propagation guarantee is self-contained.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `actions/upload-artifact` and `actions/download-artifact` are pinned to compatible major versions in the updated release.yml (e.g., both @v4 or both @v3), and explicitly align the versions as part of this story's release.yml changes.
findings.finding: The existing release.yml uses `actions/upload-artifact@v7` for the build job and `actions/download-artifact@v8` for the publish and github_release jobs — a major-version mismatch the plan inherits and extends for its new SHA256SUMS-bearing artifact bundle. upload-artifact v4+ changed the artifact format/API; v7 and v8 for upload vs download is an unusual pairing that is unverified as compatible. The plan does not acknowledge or resolve this mismatch.
findings.location: Scope section — '.github/workflows/release.yml — build-once + two artifact-test jobs'; existing release.yml lines 43-44 (upload@v7) and 60, 85 (download@v8)
findings.suggested_fix: Pin both to `actions/upload-artifact@v4` and `actions/download-artifact@v4` (the current stable pair with a stable artifact format), updating all four action references in release.yml consistently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend the plan's scope to also change `signing.py`'s `_gate_commit_sha()` git fallback from `--short` to full SHA (so both paths are consistent), or explicitly define and document the normalization rule that allows mixing short-SHA (live-git) and full-SHA (baked) in gate_code_version() output — and add a test that asserts the mixed-format scenario is handled correctly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'any signing.py display/match code is confirmed to accept the full SHA' (What section, Canonical commit format bullet) but provides no evidence or pointer. The codebase shows signing.py's _gate_commit_sha() still uses `git rev-parse --short HEAD` (line 301), producing a 7-char short SHA on the live-git path, while the new REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT path bakes a 40-char full SHA. The two paths will produce structurally different SHA formats that are mixed in gate_code_version() output with no normalization rule, and the existing tests use monkeypatched arbitrary strings ('bakedsha', 'deadbee') that do not distinguish short from full format.
findings.location: What section — 'Canonical commit format = full 40-char SHA' bullet; src/rebar/signing.py:301 (_gate_commit_sha, --short); src/rebar/signing.py:276 (_baked_commit_sha)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Canonical commit format' bullet: 'signing.py:_gate_commit_sha() is also updated to drop --short from its git rev-parse call so both the live-git path and the baked path emit a consistent 40-char full SHA; existing tests that assert on format (regex [0-9a-f]{4,}) are confirmed compatible.' Alternatively, if keeping short-SHA for the live path, explicitly document that gate_code_version() output is not required to be format-uniform and no code performs equality comparison between the two sources.

findings.finding: The plan says the publish job 'recomputes and compares hashes against the recorded build hashes' but never specifies WHERE the reference hashes are stored or how they are passed to the publish job. If the reference hashes live inside the same artifact bundle being verified, the check is circular (the artifact verifies itself) and provides no integrity guarantee. They must be stored out-of-band (e.g., as a signed workflow output, a separate attestation artifact, or a job output variable) to be meaningful.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet; What section — 'Publish the same bytes' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the build job, emit the computed hashes as a GitHub Actions `outputs` variable (or upload a separate, independently named attestation artifact). The publish job downloads both the artifact bundle and the out-of-band hashes, recomputes, and compares. This ensures a tampered or swapped bundle is detected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific JSON schema files (by package path or glob, e.g., `rebar/schemas/*.json`) in AC2 so the 'load each packaged JSON schema' probe can be written without inspecting the package manifest.
findings.finding: AC2 requires 'load each packaged JSON schema' as a concrete probe but does not enumerate which schemas or their paths, leaving 'each' implicitly defined by whatever happens to be in the package at build time — an executor cannot write the probe command without first inspecting the package.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: '…load each packaged JSON schema (e.g. `python -c "import importlib.resources, json; [json.loads(importlib.resources.read_text('rebar.schemas', f)) for f in importlib.resources.contents('rebar.schemas') if f.endswith('.json')]"`)' or list the specific schema filenames.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC that verifies the regular-CI artifact probe in test.yml (e.g., 'make test passes with the probe present') and confirms pyproject.toml validity, or explicitly remove these items from scope and document why they are deferred.
findings.finding: The described work includes an 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI' change to test.yml, and a conditional 'adjust force-include / build config if needed' to pyproject.toml — both scoped as optional/conditional — but no Acceptance Criterion covers either deliverable. If either is implemented, its correctness is unverified by the AC set; if omitted, the plan's stated rationale ('so the defect is caught pre-release, not only in the release run') is unverified.
findings.location: Scope section (test.yml and pyproject.toml bullets) vs Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md or ADR entry capturing the build-once pattern, the REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT env-var contract, and the rationale for env-var injection over preserve-existing `_build_info.py` (including the --no-isolation dependency).
findings.finding: The build-once pattern and env-var-injection contract constitute a novel architectural decision for this project, but the plan scopes only docs/releasing.md (operational procedure) and not CLAUDE.md or an ADR, leaving no durable record of *why* env-var injection was chosen over alternatives and what contracts maintainers must preserve.
findings.location: Scope section / docs/releasing.md bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short CLAUDE.md section (or docs/adr/NNN-build-once.md) covering: (1) why build-once, (2) the REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT contract (set by CI, read by hatch_build.py, requires --no-isolation), (3) the fail-fast rule (set-but-empty raises; unset falls back to git).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the docs/releasing.md update explicitly removes or supersedes the post-publication smoke-test instruction (regardless of which section it currently lives in), or document that a separate story owns that removal.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section states that the post-publication smoke test is 'documented as post-publication (docs/releasing.md)' and is now superseded, but the plan's doc-update scope is limited to the 'Build-once section only' — if the post-publication smoke-test reference lives in a different section of docs/releasing.md, it will remain as a stale, misleading instruction after this change.
findings.location: Why section / Scope / docs/releasing.md bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Either expand this story's docs/releasing.md scope to also remove/replace the post-publication smoke-test reference, or add an explicit note in the Scope section confirming which story owns that removal so it does not fall through the cracks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retry/recovery specification for artifact download steps in test and publish jobs (e.g., use `actions/download-artifact` with a documented re-run procedure, or wrap the download step with a retry action), and clarify that a failed download is safe to re-run since no write to PyPI has occurred yet.
findings.finding: No error-handling or retry strategy is specified for artifact download operations in the test and publish jobs; GitHub Actions artifact downloads are an external I/O dependency that can fail transiently, and a single transient failure would block the publish path with no recovery guidance.
findings.location: ## What — 'Test the exact wheel', 'Test the exact sdist', 'Publish the same bytes' bullets

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the PyPI publish credential posture in the plan's publish-job specification — either (a) OIDC Trusted Publisher (`id-token: write`, `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish`, no long-lived secret) or (b) a project-scoped API token stored in GitHub Secrets — and add a corresponding AC that the `publish` job's permissions and credential scope are explicitly constrained to the minimum needed.
findings.finding: The publish job crosses the GitHub Actions → PyPI trust boundary, but the plan does not declare the access posture of the PyPI credential (OIDC Trusted Publisher vs. project-scoped API token vs. account-wide token). An undeclared or over-privileged credential on this path is the primary exploitable point for a supply-chain substitution attack, yet the plan specifies the hash-gate and job structure without specifying or constraining the credential type.
findings.location: ## What — 'Publish the same bytes' bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 (publish step); .github/workflows/release.yml (publish job)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under 'Publish the same bytes': 'The publish job uses PyPI OIDC Trusted Publisher (`permissions: id-token: write`; `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` action) so no long-lived credential is stored. If a token is used instead, it must be a project-scoped API token stored in GitHub Actions secrets, not an account-wide token.' Add a matching AC: '[ ] The publish job declares its PyPI credential mechanism and the credential is scoped to the minimum (project-only); the workflow YAML enforces this via `permissions:` block or secret-name documentation.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }}` (always a full 40-char SHA) as the primary commit source, while the existing git-fallback path calls `git rev-parse --short HEAD` (7-8 chars). The plan never specifies which length is canonical or normalizes between the two sources, so the wheel bakes a full SHA in release builds and a short SHA on developer installs — a silent behavioural difference. Downstream code in `signing.py` that displays or pattern-matches the commit will see mismatched formats, and the AC's integration test uses `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=deadbeef` (short), which doesn't exercise the full-SHA case.
findings.location: 'Fix the hook' section — hatch_build.py / `_build_commit()` + AC #3 (integration test)
findings.suggested_fix: In `hatch_build.py`, after reading from either source, normalize to a consistent length — e.g. always use the full SHA: change the git fallback from `git rev-parse --short HEAD` to `git rev-parse HEAD` (or strip/expand consistently). Document the canonical length in a comment and update the AC integration test to use a full 40-char hex string.

findings.finding: The plan sets `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }}` (GitHub's 40-character full SHA) as the env var, but the current `hatch_build.py` `_build_commit()` function returns a *short* SHA via `git rev-parse --short HEAD` (6–8 hex chars). If `initialize()` simply substitutes the env var value for the git call result, `_build_info.COMMIT` will carry a full SHA in release wheels but a short SHA in dev installs — an inconsistent format across paths. AC #3 requires 'assert the wheel contains COMMIT equal to that SHA,' which would always fail for the git-fallback path if 'that SHA' means the full 40-char form. The existing test at line 227 also asserts `COMMIT = '[0-9a-f]{4,}'` (matches both), but any equality check against `github.sha` would fail dev builds.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the hook' bullet; hatch_build.py `_build_commit()`; AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Use `${{ github.sha }}` as-is (full SHA) and update `_build_commit()` to call `git rev-parse HEAD` (drop `--short`) so the git-fallback path also returns a full 40-char SHA. Update the existing regex assertion in test_attestation_signing.py:227 accordingly (`[0-9a-f]{40}`).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
